context.description: A philosophical nonfiction discussion contrasts psychology with neuroscience and introduces research on free will. It cites Libet’s 1970s experiments and Haynes’ later fMRI-based predictions of decisions several seconds before conscious choice.
organizations: Max Planck Institute for Human Cognitive and Brain Sciences
normalization_notes: Section heading normalized from German: 'Der Tag an dem der freie Wille starb'.
Title inferred from source document path.

rows.paragraph_text: The page transitions from psychology to neuroscience as the preferred lens for the discussion. It summarizes Libet’s 1970s findings and Haynes’ later brain-imaging results suggesting decisions can be predicted seconds before conscious awareness, framing free will as a major philosophical controversy.

content.free_text: nonfiction_book_page book_body_content theoretical_discussion Neuroscience and free will discussion. Libet and Haynes are cited to argue that brain activity may precede conscious decision making.
